hypothesis: The routed soft-codebook two-stage family has now improved the frontier through `K=96`, so the next clean step is to complete the high-K map at `K=128` and determine whether the same architecture also becomes the strongest high-quality anchor.
summary: Run one parameter-only follow-up at `K=128` with `routed_lowrank_two_stage_soft_codebook_residual`, keeping `EF=12`, `signum`, `LR=3.2e-3`, and `AUXK_ALPHA=0.0` fixed. This completes the allowed high-K sweep for the current winning family and establishes whether `K=128` is another non-dominated routed point or whether `K=96` is already the better tradeoff.
self_review: This is a straightforward exploitation step with only `K` changed, and it follows directly from repeated wins at `K=64` and `K=96`. Running `K=128` now is justified because `K=96` already captured the smaller-K tradeoff; the open question is whether the family also owns the top-quality anchor. The main risk is that the gain from `96` to `128` may be too small to justify the extra K, but measuring that is exactly the point of this round.
notes_to_memory: r97 should extend `routed_lowrank_two_stage_soft_codebook_residual` to `K=128` after frontier improvements at `K=64` and `K=96`.
The `K=96` point already serves the smaller-K high-quality tradeoff, so `K=128` should now be measured to complete the family map at the top anchor.

next_hypotheses: If `routed_lowrank_two_stage_soft_codebook_residual` wins at `K=128`, treat it as the new primary EF=12 backbone across the explored K range and begin targeted cleanup sweeps on the most valuable anchors.
If it is only marginally better than `K=96`, prefer the `K=96` point as the stronger Pareto tradeoff and start targeted tuning from there.
If it clearly loses at `K=128`, conclude that the family peaks below the maximum K and prioritize follow-up work around `K=96` instead of chasing the largest anchor.
